elements.sent-2411-001: This work discusses the problem of optimal excitation of a three-level atom of ladder-configuration by light in the two-photon state and coherent light carrying an average of two photons.
elements.sent-2411-002: The applied atom-light interaction model is based on the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We characterize the properties of the optimal two-photon state that excites an atom perfectly, i.e. with probability equal to one: We find that the spectro-temporal shape of the optimal state of light is determined by the lifetimes of the atomic states, with the degree of photonic entanglement in the optimal state depends on the lifetime ratio.
elements.sent-2411-004: In consequence, two distinct interaction regimes can be identified in which the entanglement of the input state of light has qualitatively different impact.
elements.sent-2411-005: As the optimal states may be challenging to prepare in general, we compare the results with those obtained for photon pairs of selected experimentally-relevant pulse shapes.
elements.sent-2411-006: As these shapes are optimized for maximal atomic excitation probability, the results can be interpreted in terms of the overlap between the optimal and investigated pulse shapes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Two-photon absorption (TPA) is a nonlinear optical process where two photons are absorbed simultaneously by a material and excite a quantum system such as an atom or molecule \cite{Mollow1968 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In contrast to single-photon absorption, where one photon provides the necessary energy for excitation, TPA requires two photons, each supplying only part of the total energy needed for the transition.
elements.sent-2411-009: This process has a range of applications, including two-photon fluorescence microscopy, which enables high-resolution three-dimensional (3D) sample characterization \cite{Garcia2020 , photodynamic therapy \cite{Bhawalkar1997 , where excitation is confined to the focal point to minimize damage to surrounding tissues, and 3D optical data storage \cite{Strickler1991 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, due to the inherently low cross-sections of two-photon events, high-power lasers are often required to achieve sufficient photon flux, potentially undermining the benefits of TPA and posing a risk of damaging sensitive samples.
elements.sent-2411-011: A promising approach to addressing this problem exploits entangled photon pairs, which can be generated through spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) \cite{Kwiat1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: These entangled photons behave as a single quantum entity, arriving at a molecule at correlated times, which may reduce significantly the photon flux required to achieve a comparable signal \cite{Javanainen1990,Tabakaev2021,Schlawin2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Entangled two-photon absorption (ETPA) has already been demonstrated in atomic vapours \cite{Georgiades1995,Dayan2004 and Rhodamine 6G molecules \cite{Tabakaev2021 in squeezed vacuum.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Foundational theoretical work on TPA was established in early seminal studies \cite{GeaBanacloche1989,Javanainen1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: More recently, perturbative frameworks have been developed to support experimental efforts and to optimize strategies by tailoring the spectro-temporal characteristics of entangled photon pairs \cite{Raymer2021,Carnio2021,Raymer2022, Giri2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: A fully analytical stochastic framework to describe the TPA dynamics in three-level atomic systems has been introduced in \cite{DS24 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: These advancements go hand in hand with the ongoing progress in engineering SPDC sources \cite{lutz2013,gajewski2016,pollmann2024,Serino2024 and refining detection schemes \cite{Panahiyan2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this work, the framework from Ref.~\onlinecite{DS24 is applied for exploration of the TPA dynamics in model three-level molecules.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We demonstrate that the optimization of the spectro-temporal characteristics of the input light must take into account the molecular optical properties.
elements.sent-2411-020: We identify two distinct regimes of real and virtual states, defined through the ratio of lifetimes of the states contributing to the process, in which light entanglement has qualitatively different impact and role.
elements.sent-2411-021: The starting point of our consideration is the analytical formula for the probability of two-photon absorption by a three-level atom interacting with light in a two-photon state, as determined in \cite{DS24 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: We consider a two-photon state for a continuous-mode input field \cite{Loudon00 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This formula was derived using a stochastic approach in the input-output formalism \cite{GardinerZoller10,WisemanMilburn2010 , formulated within the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation \cite{Scully1997 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Note that the evolution of a quantum atomic system interacting with a wave packet of a definite number of photons is not governed by a single Gorini-Kossakowski-Sudarshan-Lindblad master equation \cite{GKS76,Lin76 , but by a set of coupled differential equations \cite{BCBC12, Dabrowska19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: For the photons in an entangled state, one obtains the set of infinitely many coupled equations.
elements.sent-2411-026: But, as shown in the paper \cite{DS24 , it can be solved analytically.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We consider a bidirectional field prepared in a two-photon state.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our results can be straightforwardly generalized to the case of a multi-directional field carrying two photons or to take into account coupling with another input field in the vacuum state.
elements.sent-2411-029: We study the time-dependent probability of the excitation of the system.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the paper, we comprehensively analyze the properties of the optimal two-photon state, i.e., a state that gives a perfect excitation of a three-level system at some moment.
elements.sent-2411-031: Any deviations from this perfect match to the atom state suppress the maximum absorption probability.
elements.sent-2411-032: The optimal state becomes a reference point for understanding the conditions of maximized excitation of a three-level atom by light in other states.
elements.sent-2411-033: We consider two-photon light states with uncorrelated photons and a state with entangled photons.
elements.sent-2411-034: We demonstrate the maximum excitation probabilities that can be achieved by optimally selecting pulse parameters, such as central frequencies, pulse widths, and the delay between the mean arrival times of the photons.
elements.sent-2411-035: We show how the parameters of light for optimal excitation are related to the properties of the atom.
elements.sent-2411-036: We also study the effect of pulse shape on the excitation of the atom.
elements.sent-2411-037: In addition, we compare the optimal excitation with two-photon light with the results for coherent pulses.
elements.sent-2411-038: We show how the effectiveness of the absorption process depends on the state of light and the features of the atom.
elements.sent-2411-039: We analyze the role of the entanglement of photons in the excitation process and show that the advantage of using the entangled state depends on the properties of the atom.
elements.sent-2411-040: We identify two distinct interaction regimes, determined by the respective lifetimes of the intermediate and final states, in which entanglement of the input state of light has a qualitatively different impact.

elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{sec:unentangled presents the effect of optimizing the excitation of the final state for the light in two-photon states with uncorrelated photons.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \ref{sec:entangled contains an analysis of the optimization of the final state excitation with entangled Gaussian two-photon states.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section \ref{sec:coherent includes the optimization for the coherent pulses.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Section \ref{sec:conclusions provides a summary of the results.}

elements.sent-2411-168: We consider in this section the two-photon state with the temporal amplitude of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq: entanstate} \Psi(t_2,t_1) = \sqrt{\frac{\Omega_{+}\Omega_{-}}{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{\Omega_{+}^2}{8}\left(t_2-\mu+t_1\right)^2-\frac{\Omega_{-}^2}{8}\left(t_2-\mu-t_1\right)^2},\end{equation} where $\Omega_{+ ,\Omega_{- >0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: The width $\Omega_{+}$ is the spectral width of the laser pulse driving SPDC, while $\Omega_{-}$ is determined by phase matching \cite{Walmsley2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-170: Note that if $\Omega_{-}\neq \Omega_{+}$, we deal with an entangled state of the two photons.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the frequency domain, the considered state has the amplitude of the form \begin{widetext \begin{equation}\tilde{\Psi}(\omega_2,\omega_1)=\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi\Omega_{+}\Omega_{-}}}\exp \left\{-\frac{\left(\omega_2+\omega_1-(\omega_{02}+\omega_{01})\right)^2}{2\Omega_{+}^2}-\frac{\left(\omega_2-\omega_1-(\omega_{02}-\omega_{01})\right)^2}{2\Omega_{-}^2}+i(\omega_2-\omega_{02})\mu\right\}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-232: We check that for small values of the $\Gamma_e/\Gamma_f$ ratio, it is important to match the central pulse frequencies to both atomic transitions individually.
elements.sent-2411-233: For large values of the $\Gamma_e/\Gamma_f$ ratio, it is possible to ensure efficient atom excitation by entangled photons even if only the two-photon resonance condition is fulfiled.
elements.sent-2411-234: In conclusion, we have characterized the role of the light state for TPA in model three-level molecules.
elements.sent-2411-235: It has been shown that the optimization of the state of light must take into account the optical properties of the molecules characterized by their spectral lifetimes $\Gamma_e^{-1}$ and $\Gamma_f^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-236: This optimization yields qualitatively different results in regimes defined by the ratio $\Gamma_e/\Gamma_f$.
elements.sent-2411-237: We described the properties of the optimal state leading to the perfect excitation, found in the form corresponding to the time reversal of the two-photon state spontaneously emitted from the molecule.
elements.sent-2411-238: It is a two-photon entangled state whose degree of entanglement increases with the ratio $\Gamma_e/\Gamma_f$.
elements.sent-2411-239: Since the optimal state is not typically realized in laboratory settings, we characterized the excitation probabilities with more feasible profiles of two-photon and coherent states, particularly those described by Gaussian and exponential functions.
elements.sent-2411-240: In the regime of $\Gamma_e \ll \Gamma_f$, we observe that the process converges to a sequence of two independent single-photon absorptions, occurring at different moments.
elements.sent-2411-241: As a consequence, better excitation efficiency is achieved with uncorrelated photons.
elements.sent-2411-242: For a growing $\Gamma_e/\Gamma_f$ ratio, we have found an increasing advantage of using entangled photon pairs.
elements.sent-2411-243: The comparison of excitation probabilities obtained for uncorrelated two-photon and coherent states indicates the significant role of photon statistics: it is important for the molecule to interact with exactly one photon per transition.
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, for $\Gamma_e \gg \Gamma_f$ the coherent excitation probability is suppressed since the state is not entangled.
elements.sent-2411-245: The results obtained in this paper can find application in planning experiments using entangled photon pairs, but clearly the model has limitations and cannot be implemented and discussed in isolation from the physical approximations on which it is based.
elements.sent-2411-246: Further work is planned to analyze the optimization of three-level atom excitation when atomic transitions are located closer than the spectral width of the states.
